turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: The second opinion I have to announce is Oklahoma v. EPA, which is consolidated with the PacificCorp v. EPA. These cases come to us on writs of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit. These cases concern the same Clean Air Act venue provision at issue in Calumet that I just announced. In an omnibus rule denying 21 states implementation compliance for how they would comply with the new EPA ozone standards, EPA asserted that any challenges should be brought in the D.C.Circuit. It explained that its role was not nationally or regionally applicable, or alternatively that the nationwide scope or effect exception apply, but hereto the challenges disagree. The States of Oklahoma and Utah as well as an energy-industry petitioners sought review in the Tenth Circuit. The Tenth Circuit concluded that EPA was right to deem its rule nationally or regionally applicable and it transferred the cases to the D.C.Circuit. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we reversed the Tenth Circuit's judgment applying the framework we outlined in Calumet, we hold that EPA's denial of the Oklahoma and Utah state implementation plans were only locally or regionally applicable, and unlike in Calumet, we conclude that the nationwide scope or effect exception does not apply. No determinations of nationwide scope or effect form the basis of EPA's decisions because EPA based the denials primarily on fact specific determinations about the particular circumstances of Oklahoma and Utah. Accordingly, these cases belong in the Tenth Circuit. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Tenth Circuit. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion conferring into judgment in which the Chief Justice joins. Justice Alito took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
